reviews.review_text:
I came into CK3 thinking I’d play a medieval roleplaying game.
A few hundred hours later, I realized I was actually interacting with a dynastic systems simulator disguised as a strategy game.
What makes CK3 special isn’t just the wars, the map painting, or the roleplay. It’s the way the game lets you operate simultaneously at multiple scales:

micro scale → individual personalities, marriages, hooks, stress, succession
macro scale → institutions, legitimacy, economic development, cultural engineering, geopolitical stabilization

At some point the game stops feeling like “winning territory” and starts feeling like managing entropy.
My favorite playthroughs were never the ones where I conquered the fastest. They were the ones where I transformed unstable feudal ecosystems into resilient high-performance states:

- replacing incompetent vassals over generations
- engineering succession stability
- using dread and asymmetric power to contain destructive politics
- building administrative momentum through culture, economy and infrastructure
- running long-term development instead of chasing short-term expansion dopamine

CK3 shines when you stop treating it like a war game and start treating it like a civilization management simulator.
What surprised me most is how often the game mirrors real organizational dynamics:

-institutional inertia
-competence vs loyalty tradeoffs
-corruption through inherited privilege
-information asymmetry
- fragile supply chains of trust and legitimacy
- the difficulty of scaling systems without losing coherence

There’s also something weirdly satisfying about zooming from:
“this knight has bad traits and should be removed from power”
to
“this empire needs a 100-year stabilization strategy.”
The roleplay layer is excellent, but the emergent systems layer is where the game becomes exceptional.
The soundtrack, atmosphere and storytelling elevate everything, but the true strength of CK3 is that it allows radically different cognitive playstyles: casual roleplayer, dynastic storyteller, min-max strategist, geopolitical simulator addict, systems thinker
And somehow all of them work.
It’s one of the few games where you can genuinely feel yourself developing better long-term thinking and systems intuition over time.
10/10 medieval governance simulator.
Also one of the best accidental management training tools I’ve ever played.

I'd compare this to the Total War Series, over that of the Civilization or Anno (or really many 4x's) like games. This game is beautifully complex and though people complain about the upfront cost and silly dlc's that they half integrate into the game and than put an absurd price tag behind the second half of the content. And yet still somehow still more affordable than their competitors.

I find it comical that they have a subscription feature, like how bad does your pricing have to be to make a subscription program.

And even after all that, I'd still recommend people play this.

I can't keep supporting this game in good conscience. I was genuinely excited when it launched and held onto hope for years, but after 5 years I've run out of patience.

The technical state is still unacceptable — late-game lag and crashes remain a persistent problem that should have been addressed long ago. Season passes have gotten more expensive over time while delivering noticeably less content and quality. Features that the community was asking for from day one didn't show up until Chapter 5, which raises serious questions about the development priorities over the past half decade.

The historical accuracy has also been frustrating, particularly with the Merchant Republic DLC. Framing medieval trade networks as 'companies' is a real misstep — merchant guilds and actual chartered companies are very different things, and the latter didn't meaningfully exist until the 1600s, well outside CK3's timeline. For a game built around historical immersion, that kind of oversight is hard to overlook.

Most of the chapters get disabled by players after a handful of playthroughs because the content doesn't hold up to repetition. The decision trees feel shallow — there's rarely more than one or two choices that actually make sense, and the rest don't even function as meaningful flavor options. The ratio of strong DLC to weak DLC is not in this game's favor.

What made CK2 great seems to have been largely abandoned in favor of 3D portraits and cosmetic improvements. Instead of strengthening the strategic foundation, the game has drifted toward half-implemented RPG elements that leave it caught between two genres without excelling at either. The identity crisis is real.

EUV honestly salvaged my interest in Paradox's catalogue — CK3 on its own has been a consistent disappointment.

Never has so many dlcs been given to so few. 




Very fun. Modding community is so great as well. Once again need to fork over a lot of money to get things that need to just be apart of the base game. Buy on sale of possible its on sale literally all the time. Don't waste your money on the dlcs until they are on sake too. Infinite amount of hours to play on though. I'm coming from playing this game on console and its been a great experience to play again.

Good game, BUT !

+ + + A great simulation. You get a complex and detalied management of your medieval kingdom. I like it.

-  -  - Note that 70% of the content what this game can to offer is locked behind overpriced DLC paywalls. 
Too much greed I see again, just like with any other Paradox game.

While this game is really good, and better than CK2 in many ways, something should be said about the amount of DLC. I feel like the base game is missing so much content, and the amount of DLC you need to enjoy it to it's fullest potential makes this a very expensive game. Still though, I love this game and will continue playing it.

I've put around 30 hours into this game now and its fair to say, its not for me

You have to really be a fan of these style of games to enjoy it, but so far for me its been really difficult to understand the mechanics and gameplay loop, I've just been left feeling frustrated.

The obvious answer is "get gud" and that's fair, but if you don't have much time to invest in this game, you'll just end up feeling frustrated and tired.
For just $10 a month, you can pay me, Paradox, and I will be your beta tester for your DLC. I think I am more than worth it.

there's no reason, in the current year MMXXVI, that a game should require an external login. 
my rating is: glows too much / 10

Crusader Kings 3 is one of the most unique strategy games I’ve ever played. It stands out because it’s not just about winning wars, but about building a dynasty and telling your own story through generations.

The depth of the game is incredible. Every decision matters, from politics and alliances to marriages and betrayals. It creates situations that feel personal and unpredictable, which makes every playthrough different.

What really makes the game fun is the role-playing aspect. You’re not just controlling a nation—you’re controlling characters with their own traits, goals, and flaws. That makes the experience feel more alive than most strategy games.

The learning curve can be a bit steep at first, but once you understand the systems, it becomes very rewarding. There’s always something new happening, and the game does a great job of keeping you engaged over long play sessions.

Overall, Crusader Kings 3 is a fantastic strategy game with endless replay value. It’s perfect for players who enjoy deep systems and emergent storytelling. 

Gameplay: 9/10 Great | Overall: 9/10 Great
Look at my hours on this game. It's too addictive. Don't be like me. On a serious note, the Crusader Kings games has always been something I wanted Romance Of The Three Kingdoms games to evolve into.

CK3 is a grand strategy game unlike any other, because you don't really play countries like in Paradox's other big titles. Rather, you play individuals within a dynasty. At first glance this may seem similar, even Victoria 3 now has portraits with leaders and modifiers, but in CK3 every decision is from the character's perspective, not the state or government. If I arrest a noble unjustly, and I have been raised to be just, I incur stress that weakens my character. I can choose to befriend or seduce, plot to murder my rivals, or even (with the essential Roads to Power DLC) relinquish my titles and lead a band of mercenaries. 

Of course, unless you custom design them in the ruler creator to be immortal, your character will die. You then play their heir, who you may have shaped or neglected, and this gives CK3 a great variety of experiences, you can go from an ambitious diligent just ruler to a sadistic alcoholic whose vassals overthrow him with the snap of a finger. 

The game isn't perfect, there are ways for even a wholly sinful and malicious ruler to survive, especially if you learn any of the game's perhaps unrealistic exploits (which for your own sake I'll save you the details of here). But the real breakthrough for people who play this game for maybe 100 hours and get bored, and well, people like me, is learning to embrace the role-play, deliberately playing un-optimally and doing what your character would. The best CK3 stories unfold organically, let me mention three of my recent campaigns below:

1. I was the spurned member of the lesser branch of the Idrisid dynasty, but as a diligent and ambitious cynic converted to the native and puritanical Almohad faith (which would historically arise a few centuries later) and eventually led a religious revolution against my own dynasty.

2. I was the son of the soon to be King of Bohemia, and my mother was the Princess of Hungary. All should have been well for me, except Bohemia uses house seniority... I may never inherit my Kingdom. Worse, I was installed by my father as King of Hungary before I was even 14, and was soon overthrown by all the rebellious nobles. Though I'd never press my claim as a just and generous man, content to a life of serving my family, my sadistic son did, and the Hungarians who tried to oppose my rule wouldn't be as lucky this time...

3. I was King of Jerusalem, and I knew I could have no kids as a leper. At the same time, I could not trust my sister, and needed an heir I could shape to lead the realm. So I did what I had to, marrying a beautiful lustful and deceitful commoner, even at a hit to my prestige, spurning her at our wedding, and watching as an heir produced himself even as I had -95% fertility. Soon enough, I died, making it impressively old for a leper, and my 12 year old heir became Jerusalem. He promptly lost a holy war to Saladin, had little left to his name, and finally 'resettled' in Byzantium, as a vassal to the new Latin Emperor.

If that sounded awesome, get the game, if it sounded awful don't. 

If you do get it, you can get the DLC pass and experiment to see which you like, but I'd recommend the following. 100% get roads to power, Byzantine flavour is okay but being able to play as a landless adventurer is the real game changer. Then next in priority would be All Under Heaven, just because unlike any of the flavour packs afterwards it actually unlocks a whole new game region and completes the map. After this, any of the regional flavour packs if they speak to you (Northern Lords (Vikings), Fate of Iberia, Legacy of Persia, and Khans of the Steppe), of those personally I think the latter two are by far the best but this is a matter of taste. Then Tours and Tournaments adds a decent amount, followed by Royal Court and if you're really bored Legends of the Dead. Last, you can get any of the events and cosmetic packs, though I may recommend just downloading event and cosmetic mods of which there are a plethora.

So go, do something great or terrible, and be a crusader king, or something!

I have over 3000 hours in Crusader Kings and yet they haven’t fixed the issue where the paradox launcher can’t even report back to paradox and that has been going on for 2+ years. Now my keyboard doesn’t work to move around the map? Being able to do key bindings is gone. Fix your game 
Not worth

one of the best games I own

just sad I can't convince my friends to play it due to the comically evil DLC milking, truly the EA of good games
I had a game where my friend kept murdering all of my children, councellors, wifes, dogs, cats, rats, courtiers, dukes, kings, concubines, and even the dungeon spoon :,(

Extremely good game ruined by the DLC model. I have thoroughly enjoyed the base game, but cannot condone this predatory DLC system that Paradox has set up. No, I will not be purchasing a $10/mo subscription to play the whole game I purchased for $50. No, I will not spend $120 for the four core expansions.

The game lacks basic things like getting ur allies to join u at a siege (they almost always stay idly by while ure spending 6 months on a siege that should really take like 2 months if ur allies werent useless) and yet they keep working on useless DLCs like abbasid fashion. Like who the hell wants that?
A  little overwelming at first but once you get the hang of it its really fun. Start as Iceland too learn the game.

This game is fun, but waaay to expensive. I initially spend more then 100 dollars on the base game and bunch of DLCs, and now after only two years I need to spend another 100+ to cath up on all new DLCs. Now im stuck in the limbo, where I dont want to play it without additional content, but dont want to spend so much money on this game eather. All in all huge waste of time and money for me.
loving this game but as it costs like 400+ USD to buy the full thing I cannot recommend it. Pirate the DLC

Soft recommend, dependent on your enjoyment of simulation games and DLC ownership.

The game is actually pretty shallow. It looks like there's a lot to it, but really once you scratch the surface, everything is very samey. This is why having access to the DLCs is especially important, as they add breadth, if not depth. Still, you can get quite a few hours of relative enjoyment from this game, maybe around a hundred or so. I recommend playing in regions and time periods you are knowledgeable about (for instance, knowing the historical characters of the Ayyubids and the Kingdom of Jerusalem in the Third Crusade start can help bring the region alive, at least for a little bit). Trying to play complete campaigns and writing AARs as you go along will probably also help the game feel more interesting.
165 blissful hours of practicing eugenics on my unsuspecting courtiers

I first played this after being disappointed with Civ 7. I think I have been converted to grand strategy. I still love Civ, but this game has so much depth! I get lost playing this, and can't stop. Well worth the price!

THIS IS A NUANCED REVIEW FOCUSED ON THE DLC (I do recommend the base game. The dlc is where i have an issue)
With nearly 5000 hours on this game, its easily in my top 3 oat for pc games. i absolutely adore this game... which is why it pains me to give this game a thumbs down. The base game costs around 50 dollars which is reasonable considering all the gameplay you get. But once you look at the price of the larger dlc, which often times costs AS MUCH as the base game... its getting ridiculous. if you want new content you're either required to essentially buy the game again or just wait and pray the dlc's go on sell. chapter 5 costing as much as the base game is ridiculous. 

I DO recommend the game to anybody with any level of interest in "map games" but the fact that larger content expansions cost as much as the base game but only offer a fraction of the content leaves me with no other choice but to give it a thumbs down. (excuse my poor abbreviations and what not i didnt care about english class in school)

I really, really wanted to like this game. Give it a fair shot. Experience what it has to offer. But, after spending 200 hours on giving it a chance, I don't think I've had a single consistently positive experience. This is a game for masochists who like to pretend their content has a noteworthy level of historical accuracy. Everything about the game seems designed to make you suffer, from the combat mechanics being obtuse enough that not even the in-game "imminent battle" predictions know what's going on reliably, to the cultural features not actually telling you any real details about what they do, to the absurd difficulty of just becoming a feudal government, to the fact that half the game's rules options disable achievements unless you circumvent that pain point with a mod.... pick literally any goal. The game will always, within the span of around three or four generations, find a way to make you feel bad about having tried to do whatever it is you started it up for.

I bought this game for $17 on sale and I still feel kind of ripped off. Also, there's a ton of functionality that just... isn't there, unless you shell out over $200 for all the 'content' DLCs. Do not, under any circumstances, buy any of this nonsense at full price.
Hoe amazing would it be if the Developers use this map for possible future Native American projects or any Indigenous cultural group.    https://native-land.ca/maps/native-land

Good game but paradox doesnt want regular customers, they only want paypigs and whales since literally no one else is willing to spend €30 for a couple of buttons and modifiers. Take a break for a while and you now have to spend the price of a triple A game just to catch up. Or you can spend €10 a month to own nothing (and be happy!). But you don't understand, they need to charge you a small fortune so that they can continue providing free updated to the poor and huddled masses ;(((((((((((((( even though they routinely get blown out of the water by modders who make things for free but oh well.

Ill change my review to positive once i become a shareholder
Fun game but ridiculously greedy. Paywalling this many features on a game this expensive is absurd.

Bang your relatives to greatness!!!! In this game, you have to ensure that your bloodline survives the entirety of the Middle Ages. Scheme, plot, wage war, and murder your way to the top, or just play as a content minor lord enjoying life to the fullest. There’s really no wrong way to play... unless your last legitimate heir dies of the plague. This game is the very definition of a Paradox grand strategy game: massive, complex, riddled with bugs after every new DLC release (and there are many), and addictive as hell.

The game is really good
I’ve been playing since the early launch. But why has it started crashing after the recent major updates?
The game is RNG pop-up simulator, which would be fine if the DLC didn't try to eat your wallet.
Complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ without DLC, do not buy unless your budget is in the hundreds.

Bang your relatives to greatness!!!! In this game, you have to ensure that your bloodline survives the entirety of the Middle Ages. Scheme, plot, wage war, and murder your way to the top, or just play as a content minor lord enjoying life to the fullest. There’s really no wrong way to play... unless your last legitimate heir dies of the plague. This game is the very definition of a Paradox grand strategy game: massive, complex, riddled with bugs after every new DLC release (and there are many), and addictive as hell.

In short, this is an engaging, visually amazing follow-up to Crusader Kings II, which offers a number of new play styles and locations. It runs well, even on a 2020 Mac, so you almost certainly won't need to upgrade your PC.

That said, a proper review must take into account Paradox's arguably predatory DLC strategy, and the fact that CK2 is free to play. Like all PDX games, the DLC are basically necessary, even if you aren't playing landless (which requires Roads to Power) or in East Asia (technically playable, but the vast majority of content is locked behind All Under Heaven). A number of mechanics are either entirely locked behind the DLC, or are otherwise barebones. That's not to say that the game is unplayable, only that you will need to pay a lot more than 50/30 USD if you want the full experience.

On the one hand, this is significantly better than CK2 (which restricts play to Christian characters without the appropriate DLCs); on the other hand, CK2 is the far more complete game (unsurprisingly, given it's finished its design cycle). Ultimately, I'd say that the two games are different enough (with DLC) to justify having both, but not so different that you'll have a completely different experience with each.

So if money is a concern, I [i]highly[/i] recommend giving CK2 a try first (it is free to play), and deciding if you can handle the somewhat poor graphics, janky play, and high difficulty; if you have to pick between CK3 and CK2, I'd say CK2 is the better choice, though more because it will run way faster than CK3. However, if you can afford CK3 + a few DLC (All Under Heaven, Royal Court, and Roads to Power are probably the most important, but you should look for more thought-out recommendations), or if you just don't like CK2's steep learning curve, then I you should absolutely get CK3.

(FYI, the CK games are more "role-playing with GSG elements" than "GSG," so be aware that, as long as you don't have a game over screen, you haven't really lost. So don't give up immediately after losing a war or title.)
My personal favorite game of all time. It takes a lot for me to burn out on this game. If you like history and interpersonal drama, you will love this game.

as a new player i got overwhelmed but after a 8 minute tutorial i understood pretty much perfectly and to any new players looking at this CK3 is a family based game rather then a focus on other aspects

Compared to CK 2 this game is pretty bland and also worryingly easy as I was able to form the roman empire from an Austrian count without having that much knowledge on the game.
Found some mechanics tedious like diseases handicapping development and the notification UI irritating with the amount of popups I was getting. The game does have a lot of pros as I found the game to run real smooth even with my mid tier specs and the man at arms are addicting to use to just eradicate armies without issue. Found stress and the traits to be mediocre as they can really limit your playstyle (as an example, in my Roman empire run pretty much all of my rulers were stuck with either trusting or compassionate making it impossible for me to use the intrigue mechanics).

Overall  this game is pretty good if you want a more predictable and less severe playthrough unlike ck2 but just keep in mind that this game can get real repetitive due to a lack of random events and also isn't all too challenging once you consolidate power.
One of the best games i have played in a good while. I usually find myself struggling with Paradox games, however, this time I ended up grasping it much more

Compared to other grand strategy games, this one you play not as a country but as a dynasty. There's a big focus in interpersonal relationships and managing succession. Unfortunately it suffers from Paradox problem of having MANY DLCs, not all of them worth the money. Concerning performance, I almost always play in speed 3 so I never noticed any slowdowns, and I haven't reached endgame in this PC yet, so cannot speak for that stage. Although I play in max settings and didn't have any framerate drop. In general it's a very fun game and by far the one I spent most time playing.

I really liked the adventurer mode: you create a custom character, pick a spawning location and just travel around the world completing contracts, making friends and taking part in all sorts of activities. The game becomes like a tamagotchi with RPG elements. Choose what to do yourself as opposed to pre-determined role with managing the kingdom. Like a freelancer - doing what’s fun and aligned with you.

If you like history, role playing games, and chaotic things out of your control, this might be the game for you. I can always come back to it and have a new experience, no matter how many times I play it.

Played CK2 extensively, EU2, 3, and 4 extensively. This game is buggy as hell, full of needless, constant 'gotchas'. The game Ai suffers from severe mania and OCD. Automated raiding and bartering is a total joke and stops working all together forcing you into horrendous tedium. The devs royally botched domain limits in patch 1.19. It is completely inconsistent, and even if you spec into something that should increase it, it stays locked. One ruler will have it increase by one for becoming a king, but their successor will have it drop down again for no reason. It seems like with each patch the game became more 'unbalanced' (I'm guessing they're catering to multiplayer instead of single player) and their solution was to fight unbalance with even more unbalance. For real, I play these kind of games to get away from online and MMO gaming bullcrap where the game is too complex for the devs to keep up and they can't ever balance their game but have to continually pump out trash DLC that just makes it worse. In one run I had an heir, clear as day, zero reasons for him not to be and the game kept insisting I had no heir in my dynasty. This heir even had a son, of my dynasty. No sense, no explanation. Just an overabundance of useless, superfluous, contradicting mechanics designed to throw constant gotcha crap to keep the game "interesting". All the guides and video are also out of date. Folks more or less stopped producing guiding content for this game about 3 years ago, but the devs have knocked the game board off the table so many times since then that almost all the older content is completely out of date. Take the religious tenet 'Communion' for example. Totally does not work how it used to; I'll set it up like they suggest, zero indulgences, zero excommunications even though everyone, EVERYONE, in my realm has converted and everything is a sin.
strategy game at the start its overwhelming watch 1-3 tutorials and play it super easy so you understand the game if you play normal you will have a lot of rebelions

CK3 doesn't just focus on the normal grand strategy like other Paradox games, it truly wants to innovate the formula. Now with Silk & Silver, they want to add the gameplay option to play as merchants?! Wow. And they're going to add a DLC where you can play as the pope. 

What's next? I get to play as my horse?

i took some time off for short term disability back in february and this was the game that made me fall in love with the steam deck. I’d been playing Hearts of Iron 4 on it and saw it on sale and have been addicted ever since.  10000000000 Glorious Roman Empires Restored/10

Best strategy of Paradox ever. All this dramma happens throughout a game is holding you to continue playing every hours. It's like for personal connection instead of abstract ruler of a country like in EU series or Victoria

suprisingly affective timestealer, i really enjoy it 
...even if the Paradox's ussual dlc prices are pretty creazy as always

I always liked the idea of the Crusader Kings franchise, but i always bounced off of it 
Tried the first crusader kings decades ago, did not get on with it at all...
Tried the second one, and had some success with it, but in the end it was just to dense or unintuative for me...
But three, three is so easy to get into, and while it is still a grand strategy game, its interface is so much simpler, it is much better at explaning its systems, and that really does it all for me ^_^

i will be getting some of those crazy expensive dlc's, when they are more affordable, and i would advise anyone who are interested, or who like the game to do the same

10/10

I've had this game since it first came out. It's continually replayable, paradox has done a good job in keep up with DLC's improving the game every few months.

I love starting out as a small custom count and building up my dynasty for 500 years. Thank you Paradox.

I loved CK2 and I've ended up loving this game as well. It's different, but still good. The perfect medieval RPG/Grand Strategy hybrid and no matter how much I play there's always something else I want to try. I've conquered, killed, loved, cherished and invaded my way around the world. I've built great big things, and destroyed things just as big. I've united nations, empires, cultures and religions. Yea this game is a little big magical.

If I was to highlight any negative it would be the DLCs, not in their entirety but there are problems as usual with the paradox DLC model. The game is entirely playable and enjoyable base game but there are some mechanics and features that ended up locked behind DLCs that really should have been part of the core package and the game can feel a bit strange to play with out them. Not unplayable, not broken, but a bit weird and once you have access to them it's a bit annoying that they weren't core. Most of these are locked behind the tours & tournaments, royal court and wards & wardens DLC and my main message to Paradox would be that a lot of the features you added to those DLCs should have been added to the core game. People are more likely to buy your games if they don't feel like you are squeezing every last penny out of them and that is the reputation you have created for yourselves, not unfairly either. My normal response to a new Paradox release is to wait and see how it's doing in a few years and that's exactly what I ended up doing with CK3, because I don't trust you. Fortunately in the end I gave it a go and I do not regret the purchase. 

If you have similar feelings towards Paradox releases then, in my opinion, CK3 is far from the biggest offender in the Paradox library, but it is still an offender. That being said, most of the other DLCs are more what you'd expect from a DLC, more focused, completely new and very specific mechanics and areas of the map being affected, some are good and are very much worth checking out if you want, some are meh, check the reviews and don't relent to FOMO if you don't want to.

On a more positive side the modding community for this game is incredible and there are some total conversion mods for CK3 that stand up against the core game. Even if you just want to buy the core game and have fun with it, you can do that and you won't run out of things to do. I highly recommend checking out the game of thrones (AGOT), elder scrolls (elder kings 2) and "After the End" mods. They are absolutely incredible. I'm also positive there are other incredible mods I am yet to try.

This game is an easy recommend from me.
all focus on people, very hard, tons of ways to play, best time killer game i played
This is a very interesting game. Very complex, so can be overwhelming at times.
As a person with autism, this is the perfect combo of a spreadsheet sim and historical fiction. I highly recommend this game to anybody with the history 'tism.
it takes 100 hours to get through the tutorial. Not really, but you should play it for that long before you make your own game.

The game is good, basically medieval sims, but if you get into it you really quickly realize the entire game is a shell and you have to buy like 10 dlc to actually have things happen, just buy all under heaven, roads to power, and maybe the tournaments one if you like the idea of your character going to one. Don't buy coronations, it just makes the game more annoying to play. And yes the entire review is about dlc as is the game.

This is the best grand strategy game. Not only is the base game really good, the mods for this game are awesome. Notably, the game of thrones mod, elder kings mod, and lord of the ring mod all individually feel like they could be their own separate game. The only bad thing about ck3 is the massive amount of dlc, but its understandable because the game has been getting actively updated for 6 years.
the one game where i dont feel bad about not getting achivements because i can play on my super easy fun roleplaying mode

it is a truly immersive game, you can get lost in it for hours at a time.  ONLY FOR WINDOWS

for some reason when i switched to linux and tried to run the game i realized i wasted 40 dollars for this game. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ this game 100% my memory immedietly after opening it and closing itself. I have more than enough ram for this game to *run* on minimum specs yet it doesnt anymore

UPDATE: i can at least play the game on version 1.3.1 with proton runtime (scout), i have to verify my game files and clear steam cache everytime before i open the app or else proton fails to run.
Very fun and relaxing game.
Crusader Kings 3 is a very fun game, with many excellent roleplaying opportunities, and some excellent mods, like AGOT (game of thrones), that allow you to play in some of your favorite universes
Absolutely loved CK2 and now I'm wanting to play CK3. I was watching a Youtuber play it and it looked really fun. Hopefully I can make some extra money soon to get all the DLCs.

So, where to start? The best way to describe this awesome game is to tell you about my favorite playthrough. I started a cannibalistic cult led by an elf king in southern Spain. Naturally, the Catholic Church wasn't thrilled, and a Crusade was called against us.

We fixed that problem by kidnapping the Pope, hosting a massive nationwide festival, and sacrificing then eating the man.

10/10. Come for the grand strategy, stay for the Pope-flavored buffets.

Found out about CKII back around 2015 thanks to the insane posts on /rShitCrusaderKingsSay and from an Elder Scrolls mod, which later influenced me enough to get the game and its entirety during a Humble Bundle sale. From the few hundred hours I have played of it, coming into CK3 was exciting and also with some uncertainty.

To start? If we were to compare the base games together (without DLC), CK3 won by a large margin. They incorporated many of the DLCs of CKII into the core of the game, which while less in content; the framework and workshop support offered at the time was enough of a solid baseline to work with. I was worried at the time as Imperator: Rome ended up performing poorly and wasn't sure if the same track would be followed with CK3; hence the uncertainty.

As the years went on since release, with innumerous hotfixes, the expansion and reimagining of core mechanics, actually getting to play in Asia this time, alongside the upcoming Theocratic & Merchantile government types; CK3 has, in my honest opinion - beaten CK2 (outside of guilds not being a thing). It has taken them a few years to get to this point, but PDS Black has persevered through the hardships and at the time of writing this review; have delivered a sequel worthy of your time if you enjoy CK2 in the past.

Whilst the review is positive, I do feel the need to bring up a critical issue with the game, Multiplayer.
This doesn't seem to be an isolated incident within CK3, but also with more recent Paradox titles as of late. However, and I cannot stress this enough; the desync in this game is really, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, bad. I do not know if the engine itself is the problem, it didn't have these issues (at least widespread) in the past as frequently, but as of the current moment - expect a desync at every oppotunity. This issue has been consistent since Tours & Tournaments, with many MP sessions I've had with friends end as we've desynced every five to ten minutes, with sometimes almost back-to-back desyncs. 

There are some guides out there now that help address some of these issues, but when you read the amount of actions and things to keep in mind of, you'll probably come to the conclusion that MP is not worth the time. If it weren't for this one but critical issue with the game, which will effect the MP community surrounding Paradox's games, I couldn't rate this game any higher. Another but minor complaint is late game performance, but unlike some of the other games, there are mechanics used to (somewhat) actively help with that problem.

I wouldn't give this game 10/10, but a very strong 8/10. If the desyncing issue is addressed and fixed, I'm happy to raise that score to a 9/10. It is a strong heir to the crown, I just hope desyncitis doesn't kill them before their time.

I love games like this. There's a lot to learn, and it might be a bit difficult at first, but I'm slowly starting to figure out what I need to do. Even though I started with a pretty strong character, death will come, and I have to keep playing. Death isn't game over, but the beginning of something new. So far, I've only managed to unite the African continent hahaha

Absolutely Excellent if you want to have a more character-focused game or to aggressively min-max, but an absolute pain in the ass when you get to later in the game between managing hundreds of properties, controlling vassals, or most monolithic of all: [b]Actually buying all of Paradox's DLC.[/b] That's not getting into how certain things like spreading culture/religion aren't automated by default, meaning unless you download mods to do that, you're gonna have to babysit your Councilors and make sure they actually do what you told them to do.

Absolutely amazing workshop support though, the overhaul mods are almost always [i]Chef's Kiss[/i]
Goated game and the AGOT mod makes it 100x better. Infact pretty sure 99% of my time on this game was on the AGOT mod.

at the start i was completely new and have never touched this style of game, after about 5 hours and 5 youtube tutorials later i was completely addicted to the game. will say it does take the game a while to load but that might be my pc specs
For some reason only my family members have the best stats in this game. Its def a incest sim and I love it. Also the mods in this game make everyone SO MUCH better even the base game is great tho!
CK3 is so fun! I love being able to play as almost anyone from the past and the graphics are very impressive.
Great game, be prepared to sell your kidneys to paradox to afford the DLC
good game. my incredibly high amount of hours is because of time spent trying to make mods work...

Beware.  This game is not finished.  They update extremely often, and do not save even half of their versions.  This means that you will play a multiplayer game for several weeks and then suddenly have that game become entirely unplayable because Paradox decided to go from version 1.19.0.5 to version 1.19.0.6 without enabling players to backup to the previous version.  Play multiplayer at you own risk!!!

I feel like FINALLY after 500 hours, I can speak truthfully and honestly about my opinion of this game.

**I want to preface this by saying that over time I've added mods to enhance the vanilla experience; mostly for difficulty, realism, immersion and extra assets like clothing, armor, cultures and ethnicities etc. (Honestly love that Paradox allows these mods both cosmetic and gameplay)**
Keep that in mind but I had also played Vanilla extensively.

I have been playing on and off since maybe 2022. A lot has changed, there have been many expansions, additions, reworks and more.
I had stopped for almost a whole year but I have recently become very ill so I've had a lot of time on my hands (finally writing this review for example)

A couple reasons you should get this game.
If you love history, alternate history, numbers, have OCD, don't have OCD, Roleplaying, Looking at maps, WAR, subterfuge, politics, long term goals, fascinated by culture, enjoy city builders and strategy then stop reading and just buy this.

Love fantasy? You can get a mod that changes the world completely into A Game Of Thrones (literally the best GOT game ever made and it's a FREE mod), The Lord of The Rings or even Elder Scrolls. There's even a post apocalyptic setting and plenty more.
♥♥♥♥♥♥ sick!

Basically nothing is off limits as long as you have the means to do so. 
Unhappy with your current wife/husband? Maybe they hate your guts, are scheming against you, infertile, or you need a new alliance for conquest/political clout?
Go Henry VIII and kill them off, or request a divorce via excommunication (if your faith requires it) whether legitimate or fabricated. Some realms have concubines, multiple spouses and allowed canibalism and adultery.
♥♥♥♥♥♥' crazy.

Want to secretly be a witch and start a coven? Go for it.
Your current heir is a drunk and won't have the means to rule the realm after you die and you have another child with more potential? kill them.
Incest? It's okay... Shout out to the Hapsburgs.
Abduction? Imprisonment? Seduction? Tyranny?
Duels? Jousts? Sacrifices? Weddings? 
Need a new chief Eunuch? Chop chop...
Murder feast ala GOT?
Be anyone you want to be; good or bad or a moral grey area.
Tribal Raider?
Influential Administrator?
Nomadic Warlord?
♥♥♥♥ yeah!

I forgot to mention; your character will die, whether naturally or not.
AND everything that can be done, will probably have consequences.

The main gameplay loop is to create a family, a dynasty that will last through the ages. Its so satisfying watching your tree expand over generations and gut wrenching if they are wiped out.
Sometimes your character is smart, capable and handsome...
And sometimes they are a horrible hunchbacked idiot who everyone hates...
As much control as you have, there is an equal amount if not more things that you cannot.
That's the name of the game.

You can work your way up as a lowly count or an adventurer travelling the known world. Or begin as a mighty King/Queen.
Where and what and when? Attack as a Pagan Viking raider and claim new lands, unite China as the Son Of Heaven, Destroy and subjugate Eastern Europe as The Greatest Of Khans. Be a marginalized Coptic African surrounded by faiths that see you as the enemy. The choice is yours.

Sleep your way to an inheritance with no bloodshed, or claim your saintliness as the rightful owner of ancestral ambiguity.
Take Hold of the Holy City Jerusalem from the Muslims and Vica Versa via the namesake of the game; THE CRUSADE!
The game is very flexible and offers near infinite possibilities.

There is the constant carrot on the stick effect; with the caveat of the carrot being held by you.
There are some loose goals for you to follow provided based on your religion, culture, economy and realm; but the real fun is your own story telling and player made choices. It's practically endless.

cons:
The game is expensive; major dlc is essential
Steep learning curve
It's a map simulator, that's 90% of what you'll be looking at.


Now,
I have a not so small anecdote of what has been happening in my recent playthrough with some narrative embellishment (But honestly this is basically how it went down)

| Location: The Rus, Northern Europe. Date: 1094 
Who: Yekaterina I Bokkha. Orthodox Christian. Educated Heiress. |
After the sudden and unexpected death of my mother, a female countess of a small principality in modern day Russia, I was banished from the realm by my scheming brother who usurped my claims to the land. I wandered the Oblast with my father who no longer felt the need to stay with nothing but a dead wife. I found work as a scholar and storyteller, translating texts and settling minor boundary disputes among other things. I found myself in the Kingdom of Georgia, bordering the Byzantine empire; and fell in love with with a disinherited Armenian Noble. We had four children together, 3 daughters and 1 son. The son died of Consumption not long after his birth.
While mourning his loss, we were ambushed by a group of bandits. In an effort to defend myself; I took up an old axe and attempted to slay the leader, but I was killed.

| Year 1114:
Who: Yekaterina II Bokkha, Unfaithful Orthodox Christian, Caucus Mountains. |
Witnessing the demise of my mother traumatized me and made me question everything.
Why would the Lord allow such cruelty?
I vowed to hunt down the vile Saxon fiend yet I was young and frail.
I searched for help from something else. I found covenant with the Old Horned One of some foul Pagan Faith.

I trained tirelessly as a Warrior, emboldended by my quest for vengeance.
Earning what I could through just and unjust means.
It was all a means to an end.
For decades I wandered the land but could never find my mother's killer.

I thought of my home; which I had never seen but still yearned for deeply.
In my 30s, We reached the dutchy of Smolensk.
We had dealings and enrolled patrons with a strange people called the Cumans, who; to the manorial locals of Western Europe were a savage and barbaric folk, but we found a common cause in our travels along the Dnieper river until we returned to the Boreals of the North.
Upon hearing of my return, I was approached by my uncle; Count Vodomir 'The Frog' of Karachev. afflicted with lepresy and not long for this world.
Just punishment for his misdeeds to his family and to his people.
He was unable to sire an heir, and so emplored me to give up my wandering lifestyle and return home.

What I returned to was nothing like how my mother described it.
The land was depraved and destitute; Bled dry by the greedy nobility and forever pillaged by Baltic and Cuman raiding parties. Many folk spoke of a fearsome people from the north, descending from the treeline like ghosts before vanishing.

My uncle died shortly after, with no loved ones by his side at the end, a count in name only.
After a funeral to maintain the facade of family and legitimacy.
I, with everything learned and witnessed; got to work.

I inherited his titles, much to the displeasure of his small band of Vassals who didn't want to submit to a woman and whom feared the emptying of their meagre treasuries.
And so, I killed them. Abducted them. Banished them. Tortured them.
I restored control to the mess that was left to me.
I defended our lands against the barbarians who I had once shared bread with.
I expanded and formed the duchy of Karachev.
All in the short span of a few years.
The Populace began to refer to me as 'The Wolf' of Novosil.
My neighbours hated me, but they feared me, and the fear wasn't unfounded.
I had my hungry eyes on their prosperity inland, they grew fat while my borderland was infested with godless marauders...

Alas, I had no heir of my own; I was not a good prospect due to my age and my reputation proceeded me as a ruthless and callous woman.
Was this the end of my line? Or could I tempt one of my neighbours to give in to their lustful natures...?


"Laws catch flies but let hornets go free" - Russian Proverb

It's fun and you can do a lot of things. Some of the events though will just crush you and you'll have put a lot of hours into something for an event you just can't fight against. Otherwise it is a lot of fun.
This game is very fun and makes you really think. Mods can make it even better but its pretty good just on it own.

I have spent countless hours late at night watching a map paint itself and I have not once regretted it. I don't do all nighters anymore, but this game gets me close without even realizing it. I have used this game and some of the flavor mods and ended up planning some sightseeing around landmarks in CK. The DLCs so far have all added something fresh and interesting that keeps me coming back for another 100 hours minimum.

What every gripe about Paradox is how each DLC that makes the game worth paying is practically going to cost you the base value 10 times over. But the DLCs do feel worth it and I am excited what the state of the game will look like 5 years from now.
Definitely a top tier PDX title, combining elements of a RPG and a Strategy. Out of all the PDX def the most repayable
Legitimately bought this game for the Game of Thrones mod. Worth it. I spent 60 minutes designing the perfect Northern ruler and he was killed in his first joust. 5/5
I added my friend to the game and he ended up having a child with his sister... good game.
Very fun and engaging, keep playing it gets better too

I have about 800 hours on  this game across all platforms it is a pretty in depth game but paradox is greedy. Its a lot of things in dlcs that should be base game but you should play base game first see if you even like it. Im a history buff so its pretty cool you play for hundreds of years, pretty fun gameplay it has some bs ofc too though and some things that get old QUICK. Its a lot of ways to play so even playing in the same save file but a different person. Mods are like needed at this point for me lol im able to customize my game how i want it to be. Very in depth game also has some very neat ways to keep your bloodline (pure) ;)

like all paradox games, CK3 has a huge learning curve. If you are looking for a game to invest time in an immersive era, this game is for you. What i like about this game compared to HOI4 is it's a complete sandbox, you can set goals for yourself and mess around as you want. You can play seriously or you can just do random nonsense and suffer the consequences. 

At first the combat may seem really simple - the person with more soldiers wins, but if you look closer, the combat system is also in depth, whether that be your commanders, whether you take the risk of leading your army but risking wounds, or even death or you select one of your commanders, or how different troops counter others, mercenaries, etc

The scheme system is useful (and fun) if you play your cards right, it can be used in multiple ways, such as murdering a vassal who is opposing you, or rarely - to murder somebody to take their land instead of wasting resources.

The learning lifestyle allows you to make strategic choices on what you want.

Compared to CK2, I really prefer how its *easy to learn, but hard to master*, as a whole, the game is hard to learn, but once you get the hang of the basics its fun to play easy nations. The replayability value is great! the charecter model overhaul is also fun to see other peoples' reactions. Yes, CK2 is free, but unless you are really tight on money, just buy CK3, its a lot better!

Three things that would be better is a more in depth economy system, and an advanced tutorial as well to show more complicated things. And most importantly more freedom for DLC content, for example I should be able to be a wanderer in the base game, but most of the choices are locked behind the DLC.

Crusader Kings three is a wonderfuly made game. Its fun extreamly detailed and very very easy to mod and runs smoothly on most platforms. Im playing it on a macbook air and so far no crashes or glitches. I have to recoment the AGOT mod for this game as it makes it even better to play.
I'm addicted to this game. I'd describe it as a more historical and realistic version of Civilization with elements of the 60 seconds risk choices. Very difficult and very rewarding!
A bit of a snoozefest at times, but usually good for killing a couple of hours if you're really willing to commit to a lifetime or two.

Polytical systems for dummies vol. 1

Great game

Terrible DLC policy

The app went from functioning fine to shutting off the very moment I try to select a ruler. I've cleared out all my files, deleted and reinstalled the game again, tried clearing out space by removing other games from my device -- everything to no avail. Not much point in buying a game you won't be sure if you're even able to play

Great historical civilisation simulator.
Incredible amount of variation due to unbelievable number of moving parts in this game. Game settings can create entirely new experiences with each run, and mods can clearly go even further. Important to note that DLC adds features/content that feels basically required to play the game, but it was plenty of fun even when base game/northern lords was all that was available.
Wish it fleshed out south asia/Africa more but yeah. Both glad and sad that they got rid of the horrifying and stomach-churning death noises from ck2.

Em 2015 eu li Game of Thrones. Desde então estava a procura exatamente DESSE tipo de jogo. Simplesmente sensacional, um dos melhores que já joguei. Gostaria de ter começado a jogar mais cedo pra não ser tão noob.

This game has become kinda GOATED, honestly. I can confidently say it has more than enough content for me to finally put down CK2 in exchange for this. Highly recommend, and PLEASE get into RP with your character, it adds so much to the experience overall.

ITs great game, Really Good game.  10/10 best game. Super greatest game. Seriously though better then most other strategy games. Has RPG elements to it. I played since CK1 and it has come a very long way. CK2 was the best until fairly recently. now CK3 has almost caught up to CK2 and really has a ton more features. I miss the Societies though..  I do miss extra holdings sometimes poping up in CK2. here its impossible because the holdings are on the map. and you dont siege cities or temples..  which I do kinda miss from CK2.  but its great really.  Good Game.
one of the best games I ve ever played, it is so complex, never boring... simply top
Really gun, always happy to jump back to the game once in a while

Game is OK I guess, but the UI is terrible, sincerely the worst, half of the map layers are useless, understanding core concept is a chore, mostly due to awful tutorials. 
A lot of menus and decisions are still hidden in weird places, some mechanism, like the administrative government, don't make no sense and are objectively a failure of game design in terms of UI. The game places baseless limitations on a lot of interactions. For example why is it so expensive to change faith ? It's supposed to be an historical game, have you heard about conversion ? Since when do you need to be a parangon of faith to convert to islam ?
Sorry but I can't get over the awful UI that clearly prevent me from playing sometimes since I dread it like death.
Maybe it's better on console but I doubt it. No statistics, no clear foreign menus, no list of your vassals, no list of your past wars, am I supposed to keep a chronicle of what I did, just to remind me who is my allied.
BTW, good luck to learn who is your ally, and why you can't ally or if your ally is at war, I have a solution for that, a list. And don't tell me about the ledger, it's just impossible to search for a specific kingdom just to know if it's at war.
It's just the worst slog to find anything and do anything.
There's probably a way but I have never seen a vassal army by my side without mods.
Modders, you make the game, you deserve all the credits
Do you know what is the biggest challenge of a war ? Knowing the war objectives. Sometimes you feel like playing with a blindfold, you can barely select your armies and kind of pray you selected the good one to move, btw not splitting men at wars is a bold choice, and a stupid one too.
And what about marine warfare ? Is it some sort of elaborate joke or just laziness ? It has always been a clear weak spot of the franchise, but here there's just -nothing at all-, it's a disgrace in terms of strategy games to do such a poor rendition of marine warfare in a game released in the 2020s. Have you heard about the total war franchise ? Are they some super magic coder that could implement boat in a game that's more than 30 years old ?
Oh man, that f...king UI, I can't stop talking about that. IT'S JUST THE WORST, MODS CAN BARELY COMPENSATE YOUR POOR CHOICES, Who is the maniac that decided that the map layer should be dependant of the zoom, in what type of world live those people ? what kind of humanlike creature are you ? Do you live in the same realm as us ?
...overall, the game has its moments. 
I could make a positive review in spite of those huge shortcomings but for a game that's above 120€+, Paradox you got some of my money and thank you for your game but I would tell everyone each time I speak about your game how much you sucks.

it is one of paradoxs best a stunging grand strat game

First Paradox game. I think that maybe these aren't for me. I did not like this game at all. It felt like there were way too many options and way too much clicking. I would have greatly preferred a more streamlined experience that brings the strategic gameplay to the fore as opposed to the (what I feel was the) overemphasis of customization.

So glad that they added east Asia to CK3. It goes a long way in decentering Europe in gameplay which has historically been a problem in a lot of paradox titles; even with the Coronation release being a shaky drop i hope the game continues to improve as it has been.
The game enjoyably recreates ancient history in an engaging manner. However, it requires a powerful PC to get stable performance and for time to pass at acceptable speeds rather than crawling by.

Map Painting Autism Simulator with great role play and dynasty building.

Slow burn, but once you get the game, it's very addictive.
64 hours in the game and still on training mission in Ireland, I guess this title might take a lot of my time! lol
